title: Sources
text:
The data for this project comes from the World Bank’s World Development Indicators (WDI), accessed through the WDI R package.
Indicators used:

SM.POP.NETM: Net migration
BX.TRF.PWKR.CD.DT: Personal remittances, received (current US dollars)
SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS: Unemployment, total (% of total labor force)
NY.GDP.PCAP.CD: GDP per capita (current US dollars)

Coverage:

country-level observations
annual data from 2000 to 2023
region and income-group metadata supplied through WDI

Cleaning approach:

rename raw WDI codes into readable variables
remove observations with missing values where needed for each figure
compare countries by region and income group to preserve development context

This setup is designed for exploratory migration and development analysis and can later be extended into panel regressions, regional comparisons, or policy-focused case studies.

title: About
text:
I am Faran Abbas, a graduate student in World Economy at Shandong University. You can view my work on GitHub or contact me at faranabbas@hotmail.com.
This project studies migration-related indicators, remittance inflows, and labor-market outcomes across countries using World Bank data. It is designed as an economics portfolio project that connects migration, employment, and development in a reproducible Quarto workflow.
The repository is available here.

title: Immigration, Remittances, and Labor Market Outcomes
text:
This project explores migration-related economic indicators across countries, with a focus on remittance inflows, unemployment, and development outcomes.
Using the World Bank’s World Development Indicators, the analysis compares countries in the most recent year available and examines how migration-related variables vary across income groups over time. The goal is to create a clear, reproducible starting point for thinking about migration as a labor-market and development issue.

title: Immigration, Remittances, and Labor Market Outcomes
text:
Interpretation
This project is meant for careful exploratory comparison rather than strong causal claims. Differences in remittance inflows, migration balances, and labor-market outcomes can reflect institutions, conflict, demographic structure, opportunity, and broader development conditions, making the topic well suited for deeper economics analysis.
